An object falling near Earth’s surface has two forces exerted on it: the gravitational force and a resistive force FR that is proportional to the object’s velocity v as FR=-kv, where k is a positive constant. Does the acceleration of the object depend on its mass? Why or why not?
o: No, because the acceleration is constant and equal to g .
No, because the terminal velocity of the object increases as its mass increases.
Yes, because Newton’s second law indicates that acceleration is inversely proportional to mass.
Yes, because the weight of the object is proportional to its mass, while the resistive force is independent of its mass.

q:
Resistive force - terminal velocity during fall
An object is released from rest and falls to the ground near Earth’s surface. The resistive force exerted on the object is directly proportional to the speed of the object which results in a velocity function which includes the term e−t/β, where β is a positive constant . Which of the following statements best describes the motion of the object if it falls for a time equal to β?
o: The object will reach its terminal velocity at time t equals beta , because at this time the weight of the object equals the resistive force.
The object will reach its terminal velocity at t equals beta , because at this time the magnitude of the resistive force becomes zero.
The object will not reach its terminal velocity before reaching the ground, because the downward acceleration will not reach zero until a time greater than beta .
The object will not reach its terminal velocity before reaching the ground because the weight of the object increases as the object approaches Earth’s surface.

q:
Circular motion - to slow or not to slow
Two cars, Car 1 and Car 2, are going around the same horizontal curve. Car 1 maintains a constant speed throughout the turn, while Car 2 slows down at a constant rate. At the moment when both cars have the same speed, which car experiences a greater magnitude of acceleration and why?
o: Car 2 . The acceleration of Car 1 is zero because it moves with constant speed.
Car 2 . The car’s acceleration is the vector sum of both tangential and centripetal accelerations.
Car 1 . The centripetal acceleration of Car 2 is less than that Car 1 ’s because Car 2 is slowing down.
Car 1 . The centripetal acceleration of Car 2 is partially canceled by its tangential deceleration.

q:

The water is moving fast enough to have a downward centripetal acceleration greater than g .
The centripetal force balances the force of gravity exerted on the water.
The force of the bucket on the water and the weight of the water balance the centripetal force on the water.

Person pulling themselves up in a bucket
A boy stands in a large bucket used to lift humans to higher elevations. The combined weight of the boy and bucket is W. The bucket has a lightweight rope attached to its top and extending up and over a simple pulley that is attached to the ceiling high above the boy. The rope is long enough to come back down to his hands, as shown. As the boy pulls himself upward, he applies a force F to the rope. Which of the following claims indicates a correct relationship between F and Wand provides appropriate reasoning?
o: F is less than W , because as the boy exerts a force downward on the rope, the tension in the rope is pulling up on the boy and up on the bucket.

F is less than W , because as the boy exerts a force downward on the rope, the tension in the rope pulls up on the boy. This is in addition to the normal force the bucket exerts on the boy, thus reducing the force needed to pull the boy upward.
F is less than W , because as the boy exerts a force downward on the rope, the tension in the rope pulls up on the boy, which reduces his weight.
F equals W , because as the boy exerts a force downward on the rope, the tension in the rope pulls up on the boy.
F is greater than W , because as the boy exerts a force downward on the rope, the tension in the rope pulls up on the boy. Plus, the boy must overcome the friction in the pulley.
